description: We present femto-PIXAR, a self-supervised, physics-informed U-net for reconstructing femtosecond X-ray free-electron laser (XFEL) pulse power profiles shot by shot. Targeting weak, closely spaced pulse pairs, it addresses cases where conventional XTCAV (X-band Transverse-deflecting-mode CAVity) analysis and lasing-on/off matching are unreliable because electron-bunch fluctuations exceed the lasing signal. Using data from the Linac Coherent Light Source, femto-PIXAR learns to predict a lasing-off reference directly from lasing-on XTCAV images by masking the lasing region and inpainting the underlying electron phase space, then infers the photon pulse profile from the difference. The framework retrieves pulse characteristics for sub-picosecond separations and shows microjoule-scale sensitivity while remaining interpretable through explicit reference reconstruction, thus enabling pump–probe studies that require reliable characterization of weak XFEL pulse pairs. Our method is published here: Opt. Express 33, 31235-31243 (2025)

title: Virtual Diagnostics for the European XFEL
description: We are developing virtual diagnostics for the EuXFEL in order to provide reliable predictions of the longitudinal phase space (LPS) in the control room. To this end we are developing a surrogate model that incorporates tens of thousands of start-to-end simulations as well as signals from existing non-invasive diagnostics. We rely on an accurate accelerator model to achieve reliable predictions. Our progress on improving our model, which we have validated against real machine measurements, is presented in this contribution. Furthermore, in order to account for otherwise-unknown RF offsets in the compression configuration, we have developed an optimizer capable of predicting them, which we will describe here. Finally, we give a general overview of the progress and outlook.

title: Machine learning for accelerator design study and online tuning at NSLS-II
description: We introduce several machine learning applications for accelerator design and online tuning at NSLS-II. Information-theoretic measures, such as the approximate entropy of turn-by-turn beam time series and the Shannon entropy of multi-turn data in phase space, are employed to characterize the chaotic behavior of beam motion arising from lattice nonlinearities. Using these quantities as chaos indicators, we demonstrate that the nonlinear beam dynamics of the storage ring can be effectively optimized in both design studies and online tuning. Another application involves modeling complex magnetic structures, such as gradient dipole arrays with longitudinally varying multipole components, which are critically important for our future upgrades. We propose a general approach to model arbitrary non-conventional magnets using multiple HenonNet layers, where the magnetic vector potential is integrated to satisfy Maxwell’s equations. This method yields not only an accurate but also a symplectic mapping algorithm for nonlinear beam dynamics studies.

title: Measurement-Based Digital Twin from a Single Waveform: Toward Ultra-Fast In-Situ Dynamics Reconstruction
description: This study presents a measurement-based digital twin framework that reconstructs system dynamics from a single waveform using kernel-based Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD). Validated on kicker systems at the Stanford Synchrotron Radiation Lightsource (SSRL), the approach enables accurate real-time modeling, predictive simulation, and anomaly detection with minimal data. While the current implementation is digital, future extensions could leverage analog, circuit-based computing to perform in-situ waveform analysis and prediction within microseconds. Such hybrid analog–digital architectures promise to overcome the latency and power limits of conventional processors, enabling continuous, ultra-fast monitoring and control in accelerator environments.

title: Machine state embeddings as a new modality for large language models via cross-attention conditioning
description: Understanding and diagnosing the state of a particle accelerator requires navigating high-dimensional control system data with hundreds of interdependent parameters. We propose a novel multimodal embedding framework that jointly learns representations of machine states from numerical control system readouts and natural language descriptions. This enables the translation of complex machine conditions into human-readable summaries while preserving fidelity to the underlying physical system. The learned embeddings are adapted to an open-weights large language model via cross-attention conditioning. We demonstrate a first implementation trained on real-world European XFEL machine state data, as well as a simulated test environment. We cover the model architecture, training methodology, and initial examples showcasing the model’s capabilities in action.

title: Anomaly Detection Trial at J-PARC
description: More than twenty years have passed since the construction of J-PARC began. In recent years, troubles caused by aging equipment have become increasingly frequent. To improve an operational availability and enhancing user convenience, it is essential to adress the aging issue. This presentation reports on the development status of an anomaly detection system that applies machine learning. The system attempts to detect abnormal signals using acoustic, vibration, and odor sensors.

title: Automated Tuning of the ISAC-I Beamlines with a Digital Twin and Bayesian Optimization
description: The Isotope Separator and ACcelerator (ISAC) facility at TRIUMF supplies rare isotope beams (RIB) for a host of nuclear physics, nuclear astrophysics, and particle physics experiments. With the arrival of the Advanced Rare Isotope Laboratory (ARIEL) facility, RIB availability is expected to triple, requiring more efficient beam tuning and delivery to experiments. This work presents a pivot from manual tuning to a semi-automated approach to optimize transport through the ISAC-I beamlines. The method decouples the tuning of quadrupole lenses and corrective steerers. Quadrupoles are adjusted using the predictive digital twin Model Coupled Accelerator Tuning (MCAT) application to compute transport and accelerated beam tunes, while Bayesian Optimization for Ion Steering (BOIS) is used to perform the beam orbit correction. BOIS treats steering as a black-box optimization problem, evaluating functional values only through direct measurement to maximize beam current. By combining MCAT and BOIS, this method offers a more efficient and physics grounded tuning process for ISAC-1, with potential application at other facilities both within and outside of TRIUMF.

title: SciBmad: A modern, differentiable, and high-performance accelerator digital twin
description: Since the early 1990s, differentiable simulation codes such as the Polymorphic Tracking Code and COSY-INFINITY have proven to be valuable tools in accelerator physics. With the growing popularity of machine learning applications in accelerators, the importance of differentiability has become even more apparent. We present SciBmad, a new, differentiable, and high performance software for accelerator design, simulation, and virtualization. SciBmad, which is usable in Python or Julia, features symplectic integrators that are forwards-/backwards-/Taylor-differentiable, CPU/GPU-parallelized, and includes spin tracking and radiation. SciBmad also includes a parametric normal form library for doing perturbation theory with nonlinear Hamiltonian and damped maps. Full differentiability makes it possible to compute gradients with respect to any parameter of any accelerator quantity, e.g., of nonlinear resonance driving terms, or depolarizing spin-orbit resonance strengths. With a growing list of features and contributors, SciBmad's machine learning-enabled ecosystem aims to be a powerful tool for digital twins, and for both online and offline particle accelerator control and optimization.

title: Agentic AI Systems for Accelerator Control and Optimization
description: The implementation of agentic artificial intelligence (AI) systems in accelerator environments marks a major step toward intelligent, self-optimizing facility operations. At the Advanced Light Source, we have developed a modular agentic AI framework that links large language models with diverse operational data sources and control infrastructures. Through natural-language interaction, autonomous reasoning, and human-in-the-loop execution, the framework offers intuitive, context-aware access to complex accelerator systems. Our strategy focuses on control-system–agnostic design principles that ensure safety, transparency, and reproducibility while supporting scalable deployment across laboratories. The resulting agentic workflow—combining dynamic orchestration, adaptive planning, and robust validation—has already been replicated at several large-scale accelerator facilities. This contribution outlines the core architecture, deployment strategy, and operational experience gained from these implementations, and discusses how such systems can advance distributed autonomous control in next-generation scientific facilities.

title: Bridging the Gap Between Control Systems and Natural Language: A Framework for Semantic Channel Finding
description: As agentic AI systems enter accelerator operations, a foundational capability is the ability to reliably translate natural-language requests into concrete control-system signals. This contribution surveys and systematizes several semantic channel-finding strategies that we have implemented and deployed across multiple accelerator facilities. We present four mature approaches—(1) in-context dictionary search, (2) hierarchical agentic navigation through middle-layer structures, (3) compositional reasoning for systematic naming schemes, and (4) knowledge-graph–based semantic search—and discuss how each addresses different scales, legacy constraints, and control system architectures. These methods form a unified strategy for building robust, facility-agnostic channel finders that can be embedded into agentic frameworks such as OSPREY. Demonstrations at UCSB FEL, the ALS, CEBAF, and XFEL-like DOOCS environments show that these approaches generalize well across laboratories and can be validated through expert-curated benchmark datasets.

title: An Overview of the Some of the Recent Machine-Learning Applications at the EuXFEL
description: At the European XFEL, we have been investigating several machine-learning–based methods to support beam-dynamics simulations, machine operation, and diagnostics. Our studies include surrogate models for speeding up simulations and predicting the longitudinal phase space, tools assisting machine tuning, and ML-driven approaches for improving the robustness of diagnostic image processing. In this contribution, we summarize these activities and outline potential next steps for integrating ML techniques into simulation workflows and operational procedures at the EuXFEL.

title: Digital twin development for the NASA Space Radiation Laboratory
description: The NASA Space Radiation Laboratory (NSRL) at Brookhaven National Laboratory simulates the galactic cosmic ray space radiation environment by delivering high energy heavy ions and protons to the NSRL Target Room for radiobiology studies and microelectronics testing. The AGS Booster synchrotron delivers beams to the NSRL beamline via resonant slow extraction. NSRL tuning is difficult due to the non-linearity from slow extraction and octupoles, and the beam shape is optimized empirically by operators. To streamline and improve NSRL operations, we develop a real-time digital twin for the NSRL beam line, starting from the extraction bumps in the Booster and extending all the way to the targets. This digital twin allows users to both load live settings from the real system to the online model, and to send model suggested settings to the real machine. We demonstrate that an accurate digital twin can tremendously help improve operations at the NSRL beam line.

title: Cohort Organized Learning: Clustering your Data Without a Distance Metric
description: I present Cohort Organized Learning (CoOL), a method for clustering data without using distance or similarity metrics. CoOL works with any type of data that is compatible with a neural network. I show how it works, how to know when it is working, what to do if you do not know the number of clusters beforehand, and how to evaluate the quality of the clustering. In addition, I show how to use CoOL to detect a data distribution drift. All of these subjects will be covered without reference to the true class labels.

title: PSpice Surrogate model-Based Anomaly Detection and Forecasting
description: Fast extraction kickers such as the KFA71 are critical to the CERN accelerator complex, where early detection and diagnosis of abnormal behaviour are essential for reliable operation. We propose a surrogate-based approach built on a calibrated PSpice model of the kicker to detect and forecast anomalies. After fitting the model’s physical parameters to nominal measurements, we use it in two modes: forward, to predict the expected waveform from the operating conditions and flag deviations by comparing prediction with measurement; and inverse, to infer which physical parameters best explain an observed deviation, enabling diagnosis of the underlying fault. By tracking the prediction–measurement deviation score (residual) over time, we can forecast emerging anomalies and issue earlier warnings. We also explore continual-learning techniques to keep the model up to date under gradual system drift.

title: Full 3D virtual temperature sensor for SPS injection kicker - towards a full digital twin
description: We present the progression from proof of concept to machine‑validated demonstration of a learning‑enabled digital twin for the SPS injection kicker MKP S at CERN. A high‑fidelity thermo‑mechanical model in ANSYS provides synthetic data across operating envelopes: surface emissivity; pulse parameters; thermal boundary conditions. On this basis, we train a surrogate model using DeepONet to reconstruct the full 3D temperature evolution given some input power. The surrogate is calibrated and cross‑validated with real machine data using embedded temperature sensors and measured beam parameters. Some of the key unknown parameters of the model are inferred via a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) routine. We deploy the surrogate as a virtual temperature sensor for the ferrite with the dual objective of pushing machine performance and maintaining system safety. This enables near‑real‑time what‑if analysis and supervisory advisory control on the temperature in the whole system. We discuss robustness to operational drift, uncertainty‑aware thresholds for interlocks, and the path toward routine deployment for MKP S; the approach is reusable for other pulsed devices in the SPS and PS complexes.

title: Control of MuVacAS prototype argon injection using Fourier Neural Operators and Deep Reinforcement Learning
description: IFMIF-DONES is a high-power accelerator facility designed to provide a dedicated neutron source for fusion materials testing. In this context, the MuVacAS prototype is a test bench that recreates the last section of the accelerator, enabling validation of the design under realistic operating conditions. Pressure must be kept within strict ranges, which is achieved by injecting argon into the prototype; however, the tight margins and nonlinear dynamics make conventional control strategies difficult to tune and maintain. Here we present a data-driven framework for autonomous pressure control based on surrogate models trained on operational data, which provides a simulated environment that captures the underlying dynamics. The surrogate is used as an efficient environment to train a reinforcement learning agent off-line, enabling safe exploration and policy optimization. Results indicate that the learned control policy maintains the chamber pressure within set limits while remaining robust to dynamic perturbations, highlighting the potential of combining surrogate models and reinforcement learning to develop intelligent controllers for critical subsystems in particle accelerators.

title: Warm-Start Bayesian Optimisation for Continuous Control of the CERN PS Multi-Turn Extraction
description: Multi-Turn Extraction (MTE) is a resonance-based scheme in the CERN Proton Synchrotron that splits the beam into five beamlets before transfer to the Super Proton Synchrotron. Its performance, characterised by the splitting efficiency (uniformity of beamlet intensities), flatness (intensity variation within beamlets), and horizontal core emittance, depends on parameters such as the horizontal tune, transverse excitation frequency, and excitation gain. To stabilise performance under fast and slow variations, a continuous controller has been developed. It combines Bayesian Optimisation, acting on tune and transverse excitation frequency, with a proportional controller on excitation gain for fine adjustments. Fast fluctuations from magnetic history are mitigated using warm-start data buffers represented through a principal-component embedding of preceding magnetic cycles. Long-term tests show improved robustness compared to manually corrected cycles, marking progress toward autonomous MTE operation. Recent studies also identified a new working point with improved flatness, and Gaussian Process interpolation has offered deeper insight into the underlying MTE dynamics.

title: Bayesian Optimisation for Multi-Timescale Control of the CERN LINAC3 Ion Source
description: Tuning the various parameters of the LINAC3 ion source to maximise intensity output and ensure reproducible performance during the pulse and on a shot-by-shot level is non-trivial and often slow due to conditioning effects. A hierarchical control architecture based on nested adaptive Bayesian Optimisation (BO) was originally introduced to address the different dynamic response times of the control knobs. Building on those studies, new experiments in 2025 simplified the control approach substantially: settling times after parameter changes are now determined dynamically, allowing all control knobs to be optimised within a single BO loop. The new controller achieved efficient recovery from detuned configurations and improved stability and reproducibility in short-term tests. While its long-term robustness remains to be confirmed in upcoming experiments, these results mark a significant step toward automatic, multi-timescale control of the LINAC3 ion source.

title: Advancing LEIR Automation: From Commissioning Tools to Reinforcement Learning–Based Continuous Control
description: Operation of the Low Energy Ion Ring (LEIR) is governed by two primary challenges: commissioning beams for various ion species required by High-Energy Physics experiments, and compensating for beam energy drift caused by stripper foil degradation. To optimize beam commissioning, we benchmarked high-dimensional Bayesian optimization algorithms for the simultaneous tuning of up to 45 machine parameters distributed across the ring and its transfer line to maximize the accumulated beam intensity. Using this approach, we achieved the LHC Injectors Upgrade (LIU) target intensity in less than 150 iterations without prior knowledge. Additionally, we developed a reinforcement learning-based autopilot to compensate for energy distribution drifts by utilizing encoded representations of stacked longitudinal Schottky spectra to adjust electron cooler parameters and RF cavity phases. This autopilot successfully maintains high injection efficiency over extended operational periods and is robust to external perturbations.

title: Interpretable Neural Re-parameterization of Differentiable Preisach Models
description: Building on past work on differentiable Preisach models, we propose a neural extension that replaces explicitly parameterized hysteron weights with (i) a neural density network to output hysteron weights, and (ii) a sequence encoder that computes the initial Preisach state from excitation history. The density network learns a material‑dependent Preisach density $\mu(\alpha,\beta)$, while the encoder produces data‑dependent initial states conditioned on the excitation history, yielding an interpretable hysteresis representation in which material and history effects are contained within a single model. The formulation provides hysteron weights at arbitrary $(\alpha,\beta)$ coordinates, so the model can be evaluated on re‑sampled meshes of any resolution without retraining, and enables starting hysteresis loops from arbitrarily magnetized states while supporting mini‑batch optimization, which was previously not possible. We demonstrate accuracy comparable to explicitly parameterized differentiable Preisach models on measured magnetic data, and outline extensions such as transformer‑based encoders and further interpretability studies.

title: Data-Driven Optimization of Open Loop Control Functions at CERN's SPS
description: Minimizing beam intensity loss in the CERN Super Proton Synchrotron (SPS) requires adjusting multiple beam parameters throughout the cycle — during injection, flat-bottom, acceleration, and flat-top. These parameters depend on cycle time and are set in open-loop fashion: the function defining their evolution is executed during the cycle and cannot be modified dynamically. At present, operators optimise these time-dependent parameters manually, a process that can require hours of expert tuning. This work presents a Bayesian optimisation (BO) approach to optimise beam-parameter functions using only intensity measurements as feedback, while enforcing hardware constraints directly in the acquisition function. Tests on purposely degraded beams demonstrate the algorithm’s ability to recover operational transmission.

title: Towards Fully Automated Transfer Line Commissioning at CERN's SPS
description: Beam commissioning of slow-extracted beams from the CERN Super Proton Synchrotron (SPS) to the North Area experimental targets requires trajectory control through multiple transfer lines using corrector magnets — a process that traditionally demands significant expert intervention. In this work, we present a machine-learning approach to automate this commissioning. First, we use Bayesian optimisation combined with random search to ensure that the beam falls within the aperture of the secondary emission monitors (SEMs), which provide beam-position estimates along the line. Second, we perform online system identification of the corrector magnet polarities to ensure that we are correctly acting on the beam. Finally, reinforcement learning is used to steer the full line, acting on the correctors based on SEM observations with the goal of achieving beam centring throughout the transfer line. The implementation of these algorithms is presented, together with simulation and experimental results.

title: Deployment of a configurable, containerized digital shadow architecture
description: Numerous accelerator facilities worldwide have recently begun to develop digital twins for their machines in order to address a range of issues and to explore new possibilities, including predictive maintenance, virtual diagnostics, and the exploration of novel machine configurations. A prevalent issue in developing this technology is the need for a universal framework for describing the physical and virtual accelerators to bridge the gap between them. In this contribution, we describe an application that takes a full description of an accelerator lattice in a standardized format and generates a virtual control system programmatically. This virtual control system is connected to a simulation framework that allows the user to simulate virtual experiments and generate virtual diagnostics, interacting with the model in the exact same way as they would on the real machine. The simulation model can be either a particle tracking code, or a machine learning model. This application has been deployed for the CLARA accelerator and the ISIS virtual injector, and provides a generic schema for use on other facilities.

title: Applications of Machine Learning for Control and Optimization at the DELTA Accelerator Facility
description: The DELTA accelerator facility, operated by the University of Dortmund in Germany, provides an excellent testing environment for developing and validating concepts of novel, machine learning (ML)-based control and optimization methods. Successful implementations to date include a feed-forward neural network (FFNN)-based orbit correction method, as an alternative to the singular value decomposition (SVD)-based approach, as well as NN-based feedback systems designed to control the betatron tune and chromaticity values of the 1.5-GeV electron storage ring. Furthermore, machine learning algorithms have been employed to improve the electron transfer rate from the booster synchrotron to the storage ring (injection efficiency). Another use case is the application of convolutional, multilayer neural networks (CNNs) to analyze radiation spectra produced by an experimental setup utilizing the so-called coherent harmonic generation (CHG) principle.

title: The US DOE Multi-Office particle Accelerator Team (MOAT) project

co_authors.name: Daniel Ratner
co_authors.manual_institution: SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory, Menlo Park, CA, USA

description: On November 24, 2025, the U.S. government launched the Genesis Mission (https://genesis.energy.gov/), a national mission to accelerate science through artificial intelligence. As part of Genesis, the US DOE Multi-Office particle Accelerator Team (MOAT) project is a bold and cross-cutting effort to leverage the power of AI at DOE’s current and future particle accelerator facilities. MOAT’s overarching goal is to fundamentally transform how particle accelerators are operated, optimized, and designed by connecting data, expertise, and innovation across the DOE complex. It will ensure DOE’s particle accelerator facilities are at the forefront of AI-enabled scientific infrastructure, and that next-generation accelerators achieve unprecedented performance, efficiency, scientific, and societal impact. We will present the project in the context of the Genesis Mission, its goals, methods and deliverables, and discuss its relationships to other projects and activities in the U.S. and abroad.

title: Machine-Learning–Assisted Bayesian Uncertainty Quantification for Accelerator Digital Twin Modeling and Control
description: Digital twins of particle accelerators are used to plan and control operations and to design data collection campaigns. Accurate modeling typically requires knowledge of quantities that are hard to measure directly, e.g., magnet alignments, power supply transfer functions, magnet nonlinearities, and stray fields. In this work we introduce nuisance parameters to the quadrupole transfer functions to parametrize these effects and use Bayesian methods to probabilistically estimate their values and uncertainties by calibrating the digital twins to beam measurements performed at the AGS and its Booster at Brookhaven National Laboratory. The inference is computationally accelerated using a machine learning emulator of the physical accelerator digital twin trained to a perturbed-parameter ensemble of Bmad simulations. The result is a joint posterior distribution over the nuisance parameters constrained by the data and taking into account beam monitor errors. Incorporating estimates of these nuisance parameters into the digital twin is shown to result in a significant improvement in the quality of the model and provides error bars on the model parameters and predictions.

title: Neural Surrogates for Self-Consistent Electrostatic Potential in High-Intensity Beams
description: Self-consistent space-charge calculations in high-intensity beam dynamics are computationally expensive, even when using approximate particle-in-cell–based frameworks to solve the underlying Poisson equation. These space-charge solves often become the dominant bottleneck in large-scale simulations. In this work, we present a prototype three-dimensional machine-learning surrogate for the space-charge step: a learned operator that maps a binned 3D charge distribution directly to its self-consistent electrostatic potential on a grid. The model is trained on data generated from an existing Poisson solver and is designed to act as a drop-in accelerator for established beam-dynamics codes, without the need for retraining once deployed. We demonstrate that the surrogate reproduces the reference potential with good accuracy over a wide range of nontrivial beam distributions, while providing order of magnitude reductions in computational cost compared to traditional solvers. This shows the viability of ML-based 3D space-charge surrogates as a path toward faster high-intensity beam simulations.

title: Continual Learning for Production-Level Machine Learning in Particle Accelerators
description: Particle accelerators operate in complex environments where data distribution can change dynamically, leading to data drifts that significantly challenge Machine Learning (ML) models. These non-stationary conditions often cause ML models to deteriorate in performance, making it difficult to maintain reliable predictions in operation. The primary sources of data drifts are changes in accelerator settings and changes in equipment performance which cannot be measured directly. To bridge this gap between ML development and long-term deployment in operational settings, we identify key areas within particle accelerators where continual learning can help mitigate drift-induced performance degradation. We will provide a practical guide on selecting the appropriate method given resource constraints and desired stability plasticity trade offs. As a concrete example, we will present a real-world use case for anomaly detection to predict errant beams at the Spallation Neutron Source accelerator, where continual learning has been employed to demonstrate stable performance on drifting data streams. We will present practical challenges, lessons learned, and the results from the deployed ML model.

title: BPM Errant Beam Prognostics at the Spallation Neutron Source
description: We describe the latest work at Oak Ridge National Laboratory to transform the Spallation Neutron Source (SNS) Beam Position Monitor (BPM) phase waveform from an accelerator physics tool to a prognostic tool using machine learning. Encoder-decoder models are a natural choice for this problem, given the abundance of data representing normal operation and the relative scarcity of anomalous data. We show two key obstacles to overcome in adapting these architectures to the particle accelerator environment. First, encoder-decoder models tend to prioritize low frequency signal features over high frequency features. At the SNS facility, beam pulses have consistent low frequency features that may obstruct model training. Model testing has demonstrated that prognostic features are rare at low frequencies, meaning that the model must be coerced into learning high frequency features. Lastly, we show that the ever-changing nature of the beam combined with low frequency errant pulses makes traditional, straightforward methods of model validation translate poorly to the SNS environment and can lead to incorrect conclusions about model validity. We propose several solutions to these challenges.

title: Deployment of AI-ML Tools in the Control Room: Online Interface & Virtual Accelerator Model
description: Significant progress has been made in the development of AI-ML tools at the ATLAS heavy-ion linac at Argonne. Although all tools were successfully tested online, they were not accessible to the operators in the control room. To facilitate this, we have developed a dedicated AI-ML interface that allows the user to select the beamline to tune, the task to perform, hit the execute button and watch the results. In addition to presenting the main features and capabilities of the interface and its integration into the ATLAS control system, we will report on the operational experience and feedback from the operators. Another important update is the development of a virtual model for the ATLAS linac. A model that allows offline testing before online deployment, uses the same interface with the same online experience, and most importantly, mimics all aspects of the machine. After presenting a general procedure on how to develop an effective virtual accelerator model, we highlight a few example applications. Finally, moving a step closer towards a digital twin, we plan to add real-time online exchange between the virtual accelerator and the real machine, albeit in one direction at this time.

title: The EIC-BeamAI Collaboration: Preparing Machine Learning for the Electron- Ion Collider

speakers.name: Georg Hoffstaetter de Torquat
speakers.manual_institution: Cornell University and Brookhaven National Laboratory

authors.name: Georg Hoffstaetter de Torquat
authors.manual_institution: Cornell University and Brookhaven National Laboratory

description: The Electron-Ion Collider (EIC) will rely on unprecedented levels of machine-assisted optimization, predictive modeling, and autonomous control to achieve its performance goals. To prepare for this challenge, the multi-laboratory EIC-BeamAI collaboration has been established and funded to develop and deploy advanced machine-learning and digital twin technologies within the EIC pre-accelerator chain at BNL. Working with Linac, Booster, AGS, and the NSRL line, and associated beamlines, the collaboration is gathering operational experience in ML-based optimization of 3D beam emittances and beam polarization, and in real-time, AI-assisted decision making. These testbeds allow us to probe the integration of differentiable simulations with SciBmad, Bayesian optimization, and reinforcement learning approaches with actual accelerator operation. The resulting insights will form the foundation for robust digital twins, streamlined commissioning strategies, and reliable ML-enabled control of the full EIC. This talk will present the structure and goals of the EIC-BeamAI collaboration, early results from pre-accelerator deployments, and a roadmap toward ML-empowered EIC operations.

title: Surrogate modeling of beam parameters in PETRA IV
description: The proposed synchrotron light source PETRA IV will be able to deliver a variety of bunch fill patterns beyond the two baseline uniform modes with high and low single-bunch charge. The collective effects such as intra-beam scattering and impedance, as well as coupling ratio, have a significant impact on bunch parameters such as emittance and bunch length. Evaluation of the latter is performed with time-consuming tracking simulations. Full parameter space exploration for arbitrary fill patterns to allow for x-ray beamline co-design and for experimental planning based on tracking simulations is however impossible. Using a surrogate neural model trained on tracking simulation data, we can provide a tool for photon science users to explore the bunch parameters for any potential operation mode, allowing optimal exploitation of the facility.

title: SMOCS – JLab’s Streaming Monitoring Optimization Control System
description: Currently, there exists a significant overhead for deployment of Machine Learning (ML) models in particle accelerator operations. This is due to many different custom interfaces each complex may have, the conversions required between non standard data formats, and ML infrastructure required for continuous adaptation of deployed models. To minimize this overhead, we present SMOCS (Streaming Monitoring Optimization and Control System), a Kafka-based containerized framework that enables dynamic deployment of specialized agents ranging from sensor monitoring and visualization to ML driven diagnostic and controls. This platform agnostic solution links live streamed accelerator data with custom AI/ML applications and aims to avoid rebuilding the equivalent infrastructure for each facility. SMOCS addresses the growing need for a dynamic streaming framework capable of agentic workflows in scientific facilities while maintaining the flexibility to adapt to diverse experimental requirements and the reliability required by these complex systems. SMOCS is publicly available as open source software with documentation to facilitate adoption across the scientific community.

title: From SCADA to Agentic Accelerators: Enabling AI-Driven Automation in Legacy Control Systems via MCP
description: Particle accelerator control systems have evolved from SCADA to distributed architectures, but AI/ML integration remains hindered by rigid interfaces. DESY’s DOOCS, with its C++/OO and RPC design (now extended with 0MQ), offers a robust foundation for AI-driven automation. We embed the Model Context Protocol (MCP)—a standardized, tool-oriented interface—into DOOCS, bridging legacy systems with AI workflows (e.g., LLM reasoning, autonomous optimization). By exposing DOOCS parameters as MCP tools, AI agents can directly query and control subsystems. Preliminary results: An LLM-based agent diagnosed beam-feedback failures, pinpointing root causes (e.g., misconfigured/failing loops) via MCP-exposed parameters. This validates real-time fault diagnosis, as shown in our FeedbackManager integration, where agents autonomously trace issues to malfunctioning subsystems. Ongoing work embeds MCP natively into DOOCS, ensuring backward compatibility with minimal configuration. This enables gradual adoption—subsystems expose MCP tools without disrupting operations—while supporting broad AI/ML framework access. The approach lowers barriers to AI-driven automation, towards autonomous accelerators.

title: LONGITUDINAL PHASE SPACE RECONSTRUCTION IN THE KEK INJECTOR
description: Transverse and longitudinal beam quality preservation throughout the injector chain is essential for achieving high injection efficiency at the asymmetric e+e- factory SuperKEKB. The injection efficiency is one of the key performance parameters influencing the achievable luminosity. Accurate information on the beam phase space, in particular in the longitudinal direction, would enable improved bunch-length control, better preservation of emittance, and more effective tuning of the energy profile for high-efficiency beam injection. As the longitudinal phase-space distribution is not readily measurable, its reconstruction has to be performed based on limited observations. In this work, we present the approach to longitudinal phase space reconstruction from transverse beam profile measurements in a dispersive section based on a convolutional autoencoder. The network is trained on tomography scans obtained by linac RF phase variation. The learned inversion can be used to obtain the longitudinal phase space distribution at the beginning of the linac.

title: AI-Ready Lattice Representation and ML Optimization for the BNL Booster-to-AGS Transfer Line

co_authors.name: Georg Hoffstaetter de Torquat
co_authors.manual_institution: Cornell University and Brookhaven National Laboratory

description: As part of the Nuclear Physics AI-Ready Accelerator Data (NARAD) project, Brookhaven National Laboratory is developing a demonstration use case based on the Booster-to-AGS (BtA) transfer line. The effort aims to establish a standardized, AI-ready representation of the BtA lattice using the Particle Accelerator Lattice Standard (PALS), which enables seamless conversion across simulation programs and aligns simulation models with the underlying ontology of the physical accelerator and its data systems. This ontology-driven framework provides a common semantic layer that supports machine learning workflows for accelerator modeling and optimization. With an AI-ready BtA model, we will explore ML-based optimization of AGS injection quality in simulation. This work demonstrates how standardized data structures and shared vocabularies can accelerate AI applications in accelerator facilities and serve as a prototype for cross-facility interoperability within the NARAD ecosystem.

title: Building CEBAF a Graph Based ML Infrastructure for AI Ready Data Aggregation at Jefferson Lab

authors.name: Kevin Brown
authors.manual_institution: Brookhaven National Lab. & Stony Brook University

description: As part of the Nuclear Physics AI-Ready Accelerator Data (NARAD) project, Jefferson Lab is developing a radiation minimization demonstration for the Continuous Electron Beam Accelerator Facility (CEBAF). This effort aims to establish a standardized, AI-ready representation of CEBAF by utilizing the existing CEBAF Element Database (CED) to create a standard representation of a subset of the accelerator in the Particle Accelerator Lattice Standard (PALS) format. This PALS representation is augmented with custom ontology extensions that map EPICS process variables to their respective lattice elements. This creates a Knowledge Graph (KG) of the accelerator that enables efficient data retrieval for AI applications and advanced modeling with Graph Neural Networks. The ontology-driven framework provides a semantic layer encoding correlations between components, control parameters, and observables, demonstrating how knowledge graphs built upon existing institutional databases can accelerate AI applications in accelerator facilities and enable cross-facility interoperability within the NARAD project.

title: Flow-based generative surrogate models for efficient particle accelerator optimisation
description: Particle tracking is a fundamental tool for particle accelerator design and optimisation. Conventional tracking routines provide high accuracy but are computationally demanding, especially when simulating large particle ensembles or long time spans. As a result, optimising moderate to high dimensional parameter spaces of the accelerator settings is challenging, and real-time surrogate models are out of reach for many applications. This contribution investigates flow-based deep-learning models as an alternative. In particular, normalising flows, conditional flow matching and denoising diffusion models are applied to an optimisation task at CERN's Proton Synchrotron (PS). Their performance is benchmarked against each other and against conventional simulations in terms of accuracy and computational expense. Furthermore, real-time surrogate models are developed for a resonant excitation process with the transverse damper of CERN's Super Proton Synchrotron (SPS). This contribution demonstrates the applicability of these methods as well as the first successful implementation for loss reduction in the SPS extraction line towards the North Area (NA) experimental facility.

title: The CLEAR Facility at CERN: A Versatile Testbed for Machine-Learning Applications
description: The CLEAR accelerator offers a flexible and controllable beam environment, making it an exceptional platform for the development, training, and validation of ML techniques in accelerator physics. Delivering high-quality electron beams with a wide range of tunable parameters, CLEAR enables systematic studies of beam dynamics, diagnostics performance, and advanced real-time control strategies. A key strength of CLEAR is its ability to rapidly adjust machine settings, beam optics, and operating modes, allowing researchers to explore both nominal conditions and deliberately perturbed configurations within short experimental cycles. The facility is equipped with an extensive suite of diagnostics and instrumentation, providing high-rate data essential for ML algorithm development and benchmarking. By serving as an intermediate step between simulation and deployment, CLEAR plays a crucial role in accelerating the adoption of robust, experimentally verified ML solutions within the broader accelerator community. Ongoing and future ML activities at CLEAR aim to advance automated tuning, anomaly detection, surrogate modelling, and intelligent control.

title: Predictive Modelling of Beam Charge: Improving Stability Through Data-Driven Forecasting
description: Charge-beam–based forecasting techniques offer a powerful approach for improving the stability and reliability of accelerator operation. By analysing large datasets that include machine settings, beam-line parameters, environmental conditions, and diagnostics signals, predictive models can identify correlations and trends that are difficult to extract through conventional methods. In this study, a data-driven forecasting framework was developed to predict the delivered beam charge on a pulse-by-pulse basis, enabling early detection of drifts and performance degradations. Using a combination of feature selection, regression techniques, and model optimisation, the approach demonstrated a significant increase in charge stability and reproducibility. The forecasting model provides operators and automated control systems with a real-time estimate of expected performance, helping to compensate for fluctuations, guide tuning decisions, and reduce the need for manual intervention. This work highlights the value of predictive modelling as a complementary tool to traditional beam diagnostics and control strategies.

title: AI-Ready Semantic Infrastructure for CEBAF: From CED to PALS Knowledge Graphs

authors.name: Kevin Brown
authors.manual_institution: Brookhaven National Lab. & Stony Brook University

description: JLab and PNNL are jointly developing an AI-ready data ecosystem that exposes the Continuous Electron Beam Acceleration Facility’s (CEBAF’s) operational configuration, lattice description, and control-system channels to agentic optimization frameworks through a standards-based semantic layer. The effort integrates the existing facility-specific CEBAF Element Database (CED) with extensions of the emerging facility-agnostic Particle Accelerator Lattice Standard (PALS) to produce a knowledge graph (KG) containing coherent, machine-interpretable views of devices, signals, and regions. With this KG, CEBAF’s setpoints, readbacks, and device hierarchies become queryable using a uniform declarative graph query language (e.g., Neo4j Cypher) intents and inspectable semantics suitable for agentic control. The resulting graph-backed interfaces will allow autonomous agents to retrieve authoritative machine configurations, reason over device- and signal-level relationships, and execute tuning and diagnostic workflows without bespoke CEBAF-specific logic, thereby delivering a scalable pathway from operational data to trustworthy agentic accelerator tuning frameworks.

title: Hybrid Online Optimization for Hands-Off Tuning of the ALS Injector
description: Reliable hands-off injector operation calls for fast, sample-efficient tuning under drift and competing goals (e.g., capture efficiency, energy spread, transmitted charge). We present an autotuning framework for the ALS injector combining complementary online optimizers for robust performance under strict machine-protection/operability constraints. The controller alternates methods based on objective structure and information gain, fusing diagnostics across longitudinal and transverse systems. Building on prior Bayesian and multi-objective optimization, we add extensions for tracking a moving Pareto front during drifts, time-decayed learning for stability, and global-exploration bursts to escape trade-off plateaus. On the ALS linac, we target figures of merit tied to bottlenecks (e.g., controlling beam loading-driven energy spread challenging the booster acceptance) and enforce safety via bounded steps and surrogate constraints. Initial studies show shorter tuning time and improved repeatability vs. single-method baselines while preserving capture within the booster ring’s tight longitudinal window; we summarize architecture, decision logic, and portability.

title: Physics-Informed Deep Learning Model for Longitudinal Phase-Space Reconstruction
description: Accurate knowledge of the longitudinal phase-space distribution is crucial for monitoring beam stability and optimizing machine performance. Moreover, precise estimation of the longitudinal momentum spread is a prerequisite for reliable transverse emittance measurements, as dispersion couples the energy spread into the transverse plane. Conventional tomography methods, which rely on particle tracking with iterative back-projection, are computationally demanding and require detailed measurements of beam evolution over many turns. This work presents a physics-informed deep learning approach that accurately reconstructs the longitudinal phase space in a single forward pass prediction, enabling real-time and efficient computation while significantly reducing data requirements compared to conventional algorithms. The model receives normalized voltage and synchrotron tune to enhance generalization across different machines and embeds the underlying beam dynamics to ensure physics-consistent predictions. The proposed method can seamlessly predict key operational parameters from measured data, enabling the analysis of longitudinal motion.

presentation_materials.filename: Physics-Informed Deep Learning Model for Longitudinal Phase-Space Reconstruction.pptx

title: Safety and security of ML/AI for accelerators

speakers.name: Kevin Brown
speakers.manual_institution: Brookhaven National Lab. & Stony Brook University

authors.name: Kevin Brown
authors.manual_institution: Brookhaven National Lab. & Stony Brook University

description: The use of ML and AI in accelerator control systems has great benefits for improving optimization, configuration, and maintaining high performance. Methods, such as Bayesian optimization of Gaussian processes have proved effective, as have reinforcement learning methods that have longer term memory. As the needs to improve the control and automation over larger sets of parameters have increased, so have the complexity and sophistication of the AI methods. We currently are adding agentic AI, foundation models, and digital twins to our suite of tools for improved controls, which bring in better control over complex problems, but are more complex themselves. We have not delved deeply into how safe and secure the methods are we are adopting. How do we ensure reliability, validate accuracy, evaluate for bias and fairness, adaptability, and efficiency. Are the traditional machine protection systems able to perform properly in an AI managed accelerator? If we use AI in our machine protection systems, how do we validate it will perform properly? This presentation will explore these questions and present ideas on how to build safe and secure ML/AI systems for particle accelerators.

title: Reproducible MLOps Workflow for Online Deployment of ML Models in Accelerator Control Systems
description: Operating scientific user facilities requires continuous control adjustments and rapid decision-making to boost throughput and scientific output. Incorporating machine learning (ML) surrogate models into the control room helps operators efficiently explore complex parameters for optimal beam time and greater productivity. We present a robust, reproducible MLOps workflow developed at SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory for deploying ML models in online accelerator control. Using the LUME-Model deployment template, our framework standardizes containerization and deployment of ML models on Kubernetes clusters for continuous inference. This includes rigorous model versioning and tracking, seamless transitions from model training to online serving, and flexible configuration for rapid adaptation to diverse control scenarios. This approach minimizes boilerplate code, ensures consistency, and reduces operational overhead, enabling scientists and engineers to focus on model development and validation. This approach, prototyped with two surrogate models at SLAC, accelerates the integration of ML into control systems, enhancing automation, reliability, and scientific output.

title: Development of a Beam Intensity Prediction Method for ECR Ion Sources
description: At heavy ion accelerator facilities, ion source stability is one of the critical issues. Particularly in superheavy element experiments, high charged metal ion beams needs to be supplied continuously for over a month. During such operations, the ion source state changes constantly, and the ion beam intensity decreases. To continue the experiment, the parameters of the ECR ion source must be periodically tuned to keep the ion beam intensity stable. However, to diagnose the state of the heavy ion beam extracted from the ECR ion source, it is necessary to stop the experiment and measure it. We have developed a beam intensity prediction method using images and machine learning as a non-destructive beam diagnostic method. We experimentally verified this method using actual data from two ion sources: a 14 GHz ECR ion source and a 28 GHz ECR ion source. This method has various applications, such as determining the need for tuning as a monitor for beam stability at the ion source, and correcting beam intensity variations during automatic control of the downstream beam transport system. In this presentation, we present the development status of the beam intensity prediction method.

title: AqFlow: A reproducible automation framework for accelerator equipment and data workflows
description: Modern accelerator operations increasingly rely on software-defined automation, continuous monitoring, and rapid, data-driven iteration. We present AqFlow (Auto-equipment Flow), an open, modular framework that standardizes how equipment automation and analysis pipelines are designed, executed, and governed at CERN. The framework couples declarative configuration with strong reproducibility, enabling teams to move from notebooks and ad-hoc scripts to CI/CD-ready, testable services deployable on shared infrastructure. We describe AqFlow’s architecture—graph-based pipelines, development improvements and observability and show how domain experts compose analyses into safe, auditable flows. A case study demonstrates an end-to-end pipeline: running custom Python analytics for drift/anomaly detection, updating a virtual device for downstream consumers, and notifying experts via standard channels. We conclude with a roadmap for expanded device adapters, real-time execution paths, and tighter integration with model registries and scheduling systems to support closed-loop optimization and resilient autonomous operations.

presentation_materials.filename: AqFlow_ A Framework for Equipment Automation.pptx

title: Digital Twin Industry Standards and Opportunities from the Particle Accelerator Community
description: Digital twins (DTs) are predictive models of a physical system that dynamically update to reflect any changes. This concept was first conceived as early as 1993 by David Gelernter in his speculative non-fiction Mirror Worlds. DTs were coined in 2002 by Michael Grieves and applied to Product Lifecycle Management for manufacturing. Since then, industry has been developing tools to simplify the creation, deployment, and use of digital twins for manufacturing, fleet management, and biological systems. We will recommend industry standard technology and interfaces that we should adopt in the accelerator community. We further identify gaps in this technology to which we can add new capabilities and solutions, which can be expanded for our use cases.

title: Demonstration of MLOps at Fermilab
description: Designing and maintaining machine learning algorithms on live system is challenging: the dataset which the algorithm is trained on is an asset of the model, which needs to be kept track of for: 1. Ensuring that incoming live data matches the training distributions, 2. Comparing the performance of candidate models. To reliably manage large scale accelerator operations data for machine learning, we demonstrate a set of procedures for dataset management within the Fermilab accelerator complex that covers the entire data lifecycle—from acquisition to model consumption. Data is ingested using a structured metadata framework that adheres to our defined requirements, enabling consistency and standardization in data governance. Across the various stages of machine learning development and operation, we present workflows that distinguish data collection during development from data collection in production, and demonstrate how MLOps practices can reduce manual data extraction and improve reproducibility. Finally, we describe how we established requirements for our data acquisition system, data storage systems, and model framework.

title: Learning Hysteron Density Maps by Proxy Measurements at CLARA
description: Hysteresis effects in electro-magnets present challenges for operating particle accelerators. In particular, dipole and quadrupole hysteresis can have significant effects on beam steering and focus. This can make it challenging to return to consistent configuration states for user experiments and calibrations. This is typically addressed by the degaussing of magnets between state changes, though this is a costly process in terms of time and energy. Here we propose a physics informed machine learning model which predicts field strength from current history, allowing operators to return to specific configurations without the need for degaussing. This method uses proxy measurements such as dipole kick to prevent the need for direct measurement of the magnet field, which is rarely possible for installed magnets. We demonstrate the method using simulated and real data from the CLARA accelerator at Daresbury.

title: SYNAPSE: An open-source platform for real-time guidance to accelerator operation, based on HPC simulations and ML surrogates
description: During the operation of accelerators, operators must adjust control parameters and make real-time decisions to optimize performance. Implementing machine learning algorithms that learn from prior experiments and simulations can assist operators in navigating the parameter space, leading to more effective use of limited beam time. To tackle this issue, we built a platform to connect multi-GPU simulations at NERSC with real-time data from an accelerator and enable on-demand training of ML surrogates from this combined dataset. The predictions generated by the ML models are presented in the control room to support operational decision-making. This platform has been deployed at LBNL’s BELLA facility and can be adapted for other facilities. Our open-source framework incorporates various infrastructure elements to facilitate this workflow, such as Docker containers for portable application processes, the NERSC SuperFacility API for real-time job submission, NERSC Spin for the deployment of a web-based dashboard, and lume-model as a standardized interface to ML models. Future enhancements to some of these components are planned under the American Science Cloud initiative.

title: Robust Deep Learning Surrogate for 2D Transient Coherent Synchrotron Radiation

authors.name: Indranil Nayak
authors.manual_institution: SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory, Stanford University

co_authors.name: Jingyi Tang
co_authors.manual_institution: SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory, Stanford University

co_authors.name: Mr Obed Camacho
co_authors.manual_institution: SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory, Stanford University

co_authors.name: Mr Nathan Majernik
co_authors.manual_institution: SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory, Stanford University

co_authors.name: Mr River Robles
co_authors.manual_institution: SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory, Stanford University

description: The design and optimization of modern accelerators rely heavily on accurate modeling of collective effects such as coherent synchrotron radiation (CSR). CSR effects can become exceptionally strong in high-brightness facilities such as FACET-II, where ultrashort, high-peak-current electron bunches traverse beam-compressing arcs. Under these conditions, the radiation fields develop significant transverse–longitudinal structure, making traditional 1D CSR models insufficient. Computing full 2D transient CSR wakes in particle-tracking simulations is computationally demanding, often limiting downstream applications that require large simulation ensembles. We present a robust convolutional neural network (CNN) surrogate trained across varying spatio-temporal grid resolutions to predict full 2D transient CSR wakes, capturing both longitudinal and transverse components as they evolve through the dipole. The model closely reproduces high-fidelity simulation results while providing orders-of-magnitude speedup over standard CSR solvers. This fast surrogate enables efficient design, control, optimization, and uncertainty quantification for next-generation high-brightness accelerators.

title: Longitudinal beam tracking simulation as an environment of deep reinforcement learning

speakers.name: Dr Fumihiko Tamura
speakers.manual_institution: J-PARC Center, Japan Atomic Energy Agency

authors.name: Dr Fumihiko Tamura
authors.manual_institution: J-PARC Center, Japan Atomic Energy Agency

description: In a proton synchrotron, the rf system has numerous tunable parameters such as voltage patterns and phase and frequency modulation patterns. In some cases, the parameter can be determined by using analytical methods. For example, single harmonic rf voltage patterns can be obtained from analytical formulas based on the longitudinal emittance and momentum filling factor. On the other hand, several parameters that cannot be determined by analytical methods are needed to be manually tuned by observing the beam behavior in real accelerators or in longitudinal beam simulations. To reduce reliance on manual optimization and improve overall tuning efficiency, we investigate the application of deep reinforcement learning (RL) to rf parameter optimization. In our framework, an RL agent interacts with a longitudinal beam tracking simulation used as the environment. The agent adjusts rf parameters as actions, receives the simulated beam state, and obtains a reward based on how close the beam state is to the target. In this presentation, we introduce the RL framework and show preliminary results.

title: Fast FEL Tuning using Feedback-Optimisation and Reinforcement Learning at the EuXFEL
description: At the European XFEL, a significant amount of time is spent tuning the machine for experiments. One aspect is the setup of the lasing process, where the optics of the electron beam have to be adjusted to match the undulator while at the same time controlling its orbit with micrometer precision. The current solution involves two nested algorithms: an outer optimizer using the Nelder-Mead algorithm to increase the lasing intensity by tuning quadrupole magnets and an inner orbit feedback that is correcting for magnet kicks. However, since the outcome of a tuning step has to be evaluated after orbit correction, this setup leads to a small iteration rate of the optimizer. The current work investigates methods for speeding up the optimization process. As a first step, a feed-forward compensation for the quadrupole kicks is implemented, reducing the deviation from the reference orbit. Furthermore, the existing orbit feedback is complemented by online feedback-optimisation and reinforcement learning to increase its convergence speed. Simulations show that the two steps lead to a considerable improvement in iteration rate of the intensity optimizer, reducing the lasing setup time.

title: Interpretable CNN Autoencoder Based Fault Diagnosis for the Optical Synchronization System of the European XFEL
description: We present a convolutional autoencoder (CAE)–based method for detecting anomalies in the optical synchronization system of the European XFEL. The CAE is trained on healthy reference data using two-channel power spectral density (PSD) representations of phase-locked loop controller inputs and outputs, and applied to operational data to derive reconstruction-error anomaly scores. Although the CAE reliably identifies anomalous behavior, interpreting these scores is challenging. We therefore correlate them with additional time- and frequency-domain features as well as handcrafted features to reveal which physical or statistical properties change with rising anomaly scores. A dependency graph of the synchronization system further helps link anomalies to potential upstream sources. The CAE not only identifies faults but also captures long-term drifts. By relating these trends to system dependencies, we find that the most significant variations align with changes in temperature and relative humidity. This interpretable ML workflow supports robust fault diagnosis and improves the environmental resilience of the EuXFEL synchronization system.

title: Benchmarking and Optimizing Long-Term Shot-Synchronized Data Storage for Large-Scale Accelerator Diagnostics
description: The optical synchronization DAQ system at the European XFEL records over 40,000 fully shot-synchronized channels. While this enables correlated processing across many signals, the current storage layout makes long-term analysis over days to months impractical due to slow read performance. The data follow a facility/device/location/property hierarchy, and new storage requirements were defined, including fast read access, long-term archival capability, dCache compatibility, multi-language accessibility, and efficient backups. To address this, several storage formats and layouts were benchmarked with representative queries, evaluating both HDF5 and Parquet-based strategies. The final scheme stores all properties of a location in a single daily Parquet file, using Hive partitioning to improve data organization, file discovery, and query performance. With the optimized layout, read speed for typical long-term queries increased from about 5 kB/s to up to 20 MB/s, turning previously impractical workflows into a scalable solution for large accelerator facilities.

title: What Is New With Cheetah? – Novel Developments and Applications of Differentiable Beam Dynamics Simulators
description: Fast surrogate surrogate modelling and advanced tuning methods are two prominent challenges of modern accelerator operations. High-speed differentiable simulators are a capable tool to achieve both, and have been applied in many fields, including beam dynamics. A few years ago, we introduced Cheetah, a beam dynamics simulator designed on top of PyTorch with speed, automatic differentiation and machine learning as first-class citizens. Since then, 20 contributors have extended Cheetah's feature set significantly. New features include beamline elements, higher-order effects, fully vectorised simulations on parallel hardware, novel differentiable and fast collective effect implementations and more. Cheetah has been downloaded more than 40K times, enabling users to implement novel applications, including generative phase space reconstruction (GPSR), real-time digital twins, and highly sample-efficient gradient-based reinforcement learning. Here, we present an update on the features Cheetah has gained, and applications that have been implemented with the help of Cheetah, as well as a roadmap of planned future developments and opportunities for advancing the state of the art.

title: Real-time autonomous 6D phase-space tomography for LCLS-II injector monitoring
description: Characterizing the full 6-dimensional phase-space distribution of beams from the LCLS-II photoinjector is essential for understanding and optimizing downstream accelerator performance. Long-term monitoring of this distribution is equally important for detecting drifts in machine state and implementing timely corrective actions. Continuous 6D characterization during routine operation demands reliable tomographic diagnostic measurements and fast, efficient reconstruction methods. In this work, we demonstrate the first fully autonomous 6D beam-tomography system deployed on a parasitic diagnostic line at LCLS-II. Using ML-based control algorithms, the system autonomously configures and executes tomographic manipulations within operational constraints, adaptively re-optimizing beamline parameters and scan ranges in response to changes in the incoming beam. Measurements are streamed to the S3DF computing cluster, where we perform online, high-dimensional phase-space reconstruction. This framework produces detailed 6D beam reconstructions at a rate of once every five minutes, enabling multi-hour tracking of injector beam evolution with unprecedented fidelity.

title: Four-dimensional phase space optimization of beams with halos at LCLS-II
description: Precisely controlling the 4D transverse phase-space distribution of beams is critical for maximizing performance and minimizing losses in high repetition rate FEL facilities such as LCLS-II. Conventional quadrupole-scan analysis captures only RMS beam moments and therefore misses fine halo structure, preventing halo-aware tuning and optimization. In this work, we present an autonomous quadrupole-scan workflow coupled with Generative Phase Space Reconstruction (GPSR) to perform online optimization of the full 4D beam distribution. GPSR rapidly reconstructs both the core and halo of the beam from quad-scan measurements, enabling the use of emittance-related quantities as optimization objectives and halo metrics as constraints. This capability allows precise shaping and stabilization of the transverse distribution in preparation for high-repetition-rate operation with minimal beam losses.

title: Autonomous Energy Change and Ion Bunch Length Minimization using Bayesian Optimization at the ATLAS Linac
description: Optimization of longitudinal beam parameters requires careful consideration of control variables and strategic use of available diagnostics for feedback. To develop new medical isotopes, production cross section of nuclear reactions needs to be autonomously measured at several energies. In the first part of this study, we present experimental strategies for tuning heavy ion beam energy using resonators, solenoids and time of flight energy measurements. Minimizing bunch length ensures high peak beam current and reduces energy spread after acceleration enabling advanced nuclear physics experiments. In the second part, we present experimental results for minimizing bunch length using a multi-harmonic buncher. Results are presented for an optimization procedure using transmission as an indirect measure of bunch length that was straightforward to implement. To reduce the risk of damaging silicon barrier detectors used to measure bunch length, temporal distribution of the ion beam is manually measured once before and after the optimization procedure for validation. Experiments were first prepared and tested offline in a virtual accelerator model previously developed by the authors.

title: Project Poseidon: Foundation Models and Agentic Workflows through Unified Control System Data Architecture
description: Modern accelerators face data challenges exceeding manual analysis. Fragmented architectures (e.g., EPICS) further hinder ML model generalization. We present Project Poseidon, a unified platform designed to standardize large time-series analysis for Foundation Models and Autonomous Agents. Poseidon's control-system-agnostic framework includes a unified database, modular analysis engine, and facility adoption layers. This decoupling enables multi-facility data aggregation, essential for training Large Observation Models (LOMs). We discuss Poseidon's role in enabling agentic workflows to monitor large systems exceeding 50,000+ variables, detect complex correlations, and autonomously trigger corrections, shifting operations to intelligent, predictive oversight.

title: Intelligent scientific facility operations via active learning algorithms: AI/ML developments at SLAC MeV-UED

co_authors.name: Daniel Ratner
co_authors.manual_institution: SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory, Menlo Park, CA, USA

description: SLAC MeV-UED is part of LCLS scientific user facility and has enabled unprecedented opportunities in the studies of ultrafast structural dynamics in a variety of gas, liquid and solid-state systems. To remain at the scientific and technical forefront, continuing enhancements to the facility and operations are needed. In this talk, we will describe developments of closed-loop, data efficient experimentation at SLAC MeV-UED. Multi-objective Bayesian active learning was demonstrated for speeding up online beam tunings and giving trade-offs between key beam properties of interest. Bayesian Algorithm Execution (BAX) is being used for dynamically sampling the pump&probe UED signal and identify key properties of interest, such as time-zero and decaying constants, with minimal human inputs. Meanwhile, simulation based inference BAX (SBI-BAX) is being developed for autonomous sample explorations and determine crystal structure and orientation with as few measurements as possible. In all, these developments will enable autonomous facility operations, maximize scientific outputs and open new areas in ultrafast science.

title: Multi-objective Regionalized Bayesian Optimization of MeV-Ultrafast Electron Diffraction Facility

speakers.name: Minseo Jeong
speakers.manual_institution: Pohang University of Science and Technology

authors.name: Minseo Jeong
authors.manual_institution: Pohang University of Science and Technology

authors.name: Prof. Moses Chung
authors.manual_institution: Pohang University of Science and Technology

description: Ultrafast Electron Diffraction (UED) is an imaging method to study the structure of matter and its dynamics by scattering of a pulsed electron beam. Its performance depends on electron beam parameters such as beam size and bunch length, which are in turn affected by UED machine settings including solenoid strength and RF phase. However, these beam parameters cannot be optimized simultaneously due to their mutual correlations, such as those arising from space-charge effects. Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization (MOBO) is a sample efficient method that is widely used for simultaneous optimization of objective parameters of accelerator facilities, but the computational cost increases rapidly as the number of input parameter increases. To solve this problem, Multi-Objective Regionalized Bayesian Optimization (MORBO) is proposed. This method searches the next measurement point only in the trust region whose center and width vary during the optimization process. In this work, we searched for the optimization points (Pareto front) of the beam parameters at the sample position via MORBO. The parametric space consists of machine settings of MeV-UED in PAL-eLABs.

title: Longitudinal phase space reconstruction under non-linear effects: A comparative study of GPSR and tomography

co_authors.name: Moses Chung
co_authors.manual_institution: Pohang University of Science and Technology

description: Precise monitoring of the longitudinal phase space (LPS) is essential for tuning any accelerators and beam transport lines, as the initial LPS distribution strongly influences the downstream beam quality. However, conventional LPS tomography is based on linear transfer maps, which confines it to linear model prediction. Constrained by non-uniform angular sampling and second-order moments, it fails to resolve non-linear dynamics such as space charge effects. To address these non-linear effects, we implement the Generative Phase Space Reconstruction (GPSR) method. We perform a phase scan with an RF cavity and use downstream beam images to reconstruct the upstream LPS. In this process, we use the differentiable simulation, Cheetah, for linear dynamics, and GPSR for non-linear effects. Finally, we compare our method with standard LPS tomography to evaluate its feasibility.

title: Multi-Task Bayesian Optimization for Automated Piezo Tuning of Superconducting Cavities
description: Detuning of superconducting cavities is a limiting factor of the performance and energy efficiency of modern particle accelerators. To counteract the distortions caused by microphonics and Lorentz force detuning, piezo actuators are commonly employed. At the European XFEL (EuXFEL), a sinusoidal piezo excitation is used to compensate for the dominant mechanical modes of the cavities. However, the optimal parameters for this compensation are currently determined by a time consuming manual tuning process. In this work, we present an automated approach for optimizing the piezo excitation parameters using a Bayesian optimization scheme. Our method utilizes a multi-task Gaussian process model to (i) learn the relationship between piezo parameters and cavity detuning, and (ii) transfer knowledge across different cavities to accelerate the optimization process. Furthermore, we show that this approach is scalable to tune numerous cavities simultaneously while keeping tractability. Simulations as well as experimental tests at the EuXFEL demonstrate that our approach can significantly reduce the time required to find optimal piezo settings.

title: Dual-Decoder Convolutional Autoencoder for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection and Denoising in SuperKEKB TbT-BPM Data

speakers.name: Charles Ndegwa
speakers.manual_institution: CentraleSupélec / LISN / CNRS, Paris-Saclay University, Gif-sur-Yvette, France
