authors.name: Valerie Gautard
authors.manual_institution: Commissariat à l'énergie atomique - Institut de Recherche sur les lois Fondamentales de l'Univers (CEA/Irfu), Saclay, France

description: Turn-by-turn beam position monitor (BPM) data are vital for fast optics diagnostics in modern colliders, but they are often degraded by noise, spikes, and signal dropouts. We present ongoing work on a dual-decoder convolutional autoencoder that addresses these issues in an unsupervised setting. A shared encoder compresses BPM waveforms into a latent representation. Two decoders then serve distinct roles. The anomaly decoder, trained on nominal data, provides reconstruction-based scores for automatic fault detection. The denoiser decoder, trained with injected faults, reconstructs clean signals for spectral analysis. Early results on SuperKEKB BPM traces show that the denoiser enhances harmonic peaks and improves tune extraction, while the anomaly branch identifies faulty channels without labels. We outline training strategies that combine simulated and experimental data, describe evaluation metrics, and discuss prospects for integration into optics workflows.

title: Progress onPoly-Lithic, a model deployment framework for integrating models into the control system.

speakers.name: Mateusz Leputa
speakers.manual_institution: STFC - UKRI - ISIS Neutron and Muon Source

authors.name: Mateusz Leputa
authors.manual_institution: STFC - UKRI - ISIS Neutron and Muon Source

co_authors.name: Mihnea Romanovschi
co_authors.manual_institution: ISIS Pulsed Neutron and Muon Source, UKRI, STFC

description: Poly-Lithic (formerly known as Lume-Deployment) is a declarative deployment framework used within the ISIS Controls group to deploy ML models into the control system. By standardising the encapsulation of the models and auxiliary functions, such as data transformation stages and interfaces to control-system services, the framework reduces the maintenance overhead for boilerplate code and environment management. We describe current use cases and recent architectural changes that simplify extension and adoption of the library. We further outline features currently under development, such as automatic generation and evaluation of surrogate models based on archival data.

presentation_materials.filename: Progress on Poly-Lithic a model deployment framework for integrating models into the control system.pptx

title: End-to-End Emittance Optimization at EuXFEL: Differentiable-Model-Based Laser Pulse Shaping
description: Free-electron laser (FEL) performance critically depends on electron-beam emittance. At the European XFEL (EuXFEL), stringent emittance requirements are essential for accessing higher photon energies and advanced operational modes such as high-duty-cycle running. Achieving such beam quality requires solving a nonlinear optimization problem spanning both photoinjector laser shaping and gun tuning. We present a differentiable, physics-informed model of the EuXFEL photoinjector laser system that enables gradient-based feedback control for temporal pulse shaping. This method provides a robust and sample-efficient way to generate desired UV pulse profiles even under strong nonlinearity. We further outline how this laser-side optimization integrates with ongoing work on machine-learning-based surrogate models for gun tuning, forming a combined framework for end-to-end emittance optimization. The results indicate that combining differentiable modeling with ML-accelerated optimization can substantially enhance photoinjector control, paving the way for new scientific opportunities.

title: Development of an automated beam tuning method for RIBF using SafeLineBO with Monte Carlo simulations

co_authors.name: Takahiro Nishi
co_authors.manual_institution: RIKEN Nishina Center for Accelerator-Based Science

description: The RIBF is a heavy-ion accelerator facility capable of producing unstable nuclei across all elements up to uranium. By combining multiple cyclotrons in a multi-stage configuration, the beam can be accelerated to high energies and high intensities. At the same time, significant beam losses during beam transport can cause problems for accelerator operation. Therefore, precise beam tuning through appropriate magnet settings becomes critically important. Bayesian optimization is an effective approach for such parameter optimization. However, conventional Bayesian optimization may increase beam loss during the optimization process and is computationally expensive in high-dimensional parameter spaces, making real-time operation challenging. To address these issues, this study applies SafeLineBO, a safety-constrained Bayesian optimization method that projects multivariate parameters onto a single dimension while ensuring operational safety. This enables fast and safe beam tuning. In this presentation, we report on the development of a SafeLineBO-based beam optimization method using a beam-transport model reproduced through Monte Carlo simulations.

title: Meta Learning assisted Bayesian Optimisation for Accelerator Tuning
description: Bayesian optimisation (BO) has become a well-established approach for efficient particle-accelerator tuning and optimisation. However, BO with a standard Gaussian Process (GP) prior can require a substantial number of measurements in cold-start settings, which limits its practical value in operational environments with tight measurement constraints. In this work, we propose a meta-learning approach to learn informative GP priors that can transfer knowledge across different tuning tasks. We use the Medium Energy Beam Transport (MEBT) section of the ISIS Neutron and Muon Source as a case study. Based on a machine learning surrogate model of the MEBT, we simulate beam loss under varied incoming beam conditions. We then learn GP priors from these data to capture common patterns in beam dynamics. These priors are then incorporated into the BO framework, providing a more accurate initial model and enabling faster convergence in new tuning scenarios. Our results show that the proposed approach reduces the number of required measurements and improves robustness across different initial conditions, demonstrating the potential of meta-learned priors for efficient accelerator tuning.

title: Response Matrix Identification & Slow Feedback Controller Design for EuXFEL to Mitigate the Tidal Effects
description: This work presents a structured methodology for response matrix identification and slow feedback controller design at the European XFEL. The response matrix is obtained using an iterative least‑squares algorithm inspired by Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamical Systems (SINDy), with prior knowledge of zero elements incorporated to improve accuracy. To capture real‑world behavior, the system is extended from static to dynamic form by introducing an inherent delay. For reference tracking, PID gain matrices are obtained by reformulating the problem as a state‑feedback design using the Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR). The controller is implemented on a model identified from open‑loop data, ensuring consistency with experimentally observed system dynamics. Finally, to compensate for tidal effects that influence bunch arrival time and energy, two additional PI controllers are introduced. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively stabilizes beam dynamics and mitigates slow drifts, providing a reliable foundation for accelerator operation.

title: AI for Particle Accelerators: French and European Efforts
description: ARTIFACT (ARTificial Intelligence For Accelerators, user Communities and associated Technologies) is the European open-science network coordinating a concerted strategy for AI across particle accelerator research infrastructures. ARTIFACT has assembled 40+ partners across 14 countries around a FAIR-by-design, federated ecosystem. Three Horizon Europe projects have been selected for funding: TwinRISE (~10 M€, CNRS-coordinated, kick-off Jan. 2027), iRIS (~5 M€, CERN-coordinated, kick-off Nov. 2026), and SCIANCE. TwinRISE builds trusted digital twins combining hybrid physics-AI surrogates, federated learning across sites, and EU AI Act-compliant explainability, hosted on EuroHPC AI Factories. iRIS focuses on sustainability through its GreenBeam work package: AI-driven design optimisation, operation tuning reduction, and predictive maintenance at GANIL, FCC, and GSI. French contributions are central to this effort. CNRS/GANIL leads TwinRISE coordination and iRIS GreenBeam workpackage. CEA contributes DA prediction and BPM denoising, Arronax and GANIL anomalies detection in cyclotrons and LINACs, Soleil ML-based monitoring for its upgrade programme. IJCLab/LPGP, delivers surrogate models for plasma laser acceleration and lepton. This talk presents the French and EU efforts, concrete technical results, and a roadmap toward accelerator foundation models and operational AI at scale.

title: BNL Linac Emittance Optimization

co_authors.name: Georg Hoffstaetter de Torquat
co_authors.manual_institution: Cornell University and Brookhaven National Laboratory

description: Since the 80s, the BNL LINAC has delivered pulses of polarized protons for physics and proton beams for isotope production. Over the years, the LINAC has been steadily upgraded and optimized to ensure maximum output and efficiency. With over 300 independent quadrupoles and equally many rf cavities, there are obvious challenges with direct optimization especially due to the associated costs. Most tuning progress has been done with repetitive, careful, and incremental simulation of each sector using qualitative figures of merit. The historical success of this approach opens the door for the usage of constrained Bayesian optimization techniques on principal components of this large set of beam optics controls. By porting the existing simulation infrastructure into our differentiable physics ecosystem, we are able to qualitatively train a GP surrogate to predict optimal emittance transport.

title: Optimal experimental design for BtA orbit measurements

co_authors.name: Georg Hoffstaetter de Torquat
co_authors.manual_institution: Cornell University and Brookhaven National Laboratory

description: The Booster-to-AGS transfer line is responsible for efficient and transparent beam transport at the polarized collider complex at BNL and has been a testbed for optimization of injection into the AGS. One of the components of the long-term vision for a digital twin of the complex is to characterize the beam injection process, a pre-requisite of which is the ability to quickly determine the orbit through critical components of the transfer line. The traditional way to accomplish this is via beam-based alignment through each quadrupole, an extremely slow, sequential process which can easily disrupt passage of the beam. Here, we propose and test an optimal experimental design approach to identifying key components to manipulate and measure.

title: Bayesian Optimization–Based Design of the New ECR Beam Transport Line for RAON
description: Bayesian optimization (BO) has become an effective strategy for tuning accelerator design parameters, particularly when simultaneous constraints on beam quality and hardware layout must be satisfied. In this study, BO is applied to the design of a new beam transport line for integrating a 14.5 GHz ECR ion source into the RAON injector system. The optimization framework incorporates experimentally measured initial beam parameters obtained from the existing Allison scanner, enabling the search to reflect realistic injection conditions. Using BO, the quadrupole settings and beam line configuration were efficiently explored to achieve optimal beam matching while minimizing emittance growth and satisfying transmission and space constraints in the existing LEBT. This approach significantly reduces manual tuning effort and provides a systematic method to identify high-performance solutions that may not be readily accessible through conventional parameter scanning. The resulting design supports both integration of the new ECR source and standalone source testing, demonstrating the effectiveness of Bayesian optimization for complex low-energy beam transport problems.

title: Safe BO in Practice: An Operator-Focused GUI for Automated Tuning at ISIS
description: After successful automated tuning tests, developing a user interface for operators to perform Bayesian Optimisation (BO) themselves during ISIS operations has been our next target. In this work, we present a user-focused Streamlit app designed to enable operators to easily perform automated tuning. Developed in close collaboration with machine operators, the GUI integrates safe BO strategies including TuRBO and hyperparameter pretraining to ensure robust performance while reducing run time and exploration noise. Iterative design feedback and tests on the machine have led to significant improvements in usability and operator trust of automated tuning. Recent successful operator trials demonstrate the tool’s readiness for regular operational deployment. This work highlights the path from testing raw BO scripts to practical, operator-centric control room applications at the ISIS Neutron and Muon Source.

title: Automated tuning using RL trained on Cheetah simulation at DESY and building Cheetah simulation for ISIS virtual accelerator
description: Automating tuning has been an area of great interest in the accelerator community in recent years. Bayesian Optimisation (BO) has been favoured over Reinforcement Learning (RL) due to its short training time and reliability. However, RL has become increasingly viable with access to large training datasets from fast and differentiable simulation, Cheetah. In this work, we develop Cheetah simulations and tune the R-Weg section of the DESY II synchrotron using RL. RL-based tuning is significantly faster than BO during inference, and initial results indicate that it is equally competitive in accuracy metrics. We also evaluate Cheetah for the ISIS Linac and explore its integration as a backend within our Virtual Accelerator for ISIS project.

title: Critical Parameter Estimation for Accelerator Tuning Using Explainable AI Algorithm
description: We have developed the critical parameter estimation method based on the explainable AI (XAI) algorithm to improve the accelerator tuning performance. In this study, 1) We build a machine learning (ML) model using a regression neural network which predicts the beam transmittance or the beam loss, from inputs of the accelerator data including the environment data; and 2) We apply the XAI algorithm to the ML model, and estimate the critical parameters to improve the beam transmittance, or to reduce the beam loss. As for the XAI algorithm, we use SHAP. To construct the ML model with large accelerator components, we have developed the ML using GNN (Graph Neural Network). The XAI predictions were verified with the beam tests.

title: Finding Closed Orbit of a Low-emittance Storage Ring using Bayesian Optimization
description: In the early commissioning stage of a low-emittance storage ring, establishing a stable closed orbit is a key milestone. Although response-matrix-based correction is generally effective for this task, it may require manual, iterative, algorithmic tuning when unexpected machine issues arise—such as vacuum-chamber obstacles, magnet polarity errors, or strong noise in turn-by-turn BPM signals. In this work, we present simulation studies on closed-orbit finding using Bayesian optimization. We evaluate its performance under various problematic machine conditions and analyze how effectively the method converges compared with conventional approaches.

title: Optimization and stabilization of Fermilab Booster using hybrid Bayesian/RL framework
description: PIPII project will raise Fermilab Booster intensity and ramp rate. Beam losses will limit average power and are hard to simulate. Presently, Booster uses operator-guided empirical tuning. This task is challenging due to high dimensionality, multiple objectives, critical safety constraints, and drifts. We developed a synergistic suite of Bayesian optimization (BO) and reinforcement learning (RL) tools to optimize and stabilize beam losses. First, active learning was used to build a rough model. Data was collected parasitically using two novel safety constraint types – nonlinear input space restrictions (based on optics model), and uncertainty constraints (to stop bad steps/beam aborts). We then applied online multi-objective BO with scalarized objectives and fitting to improve/rebalance losses, increasing safety margins by 25%. Using BO model as a safety veto, we tried several on/off-policy RL agents for long term stabilization; SAC had best performance. We found that adding contextual (state) information further improved performance, eventually integrating key knobs like linac phase and temperature into the parameter space. Long term testing is ongoing to enable operational use.

title: APOLLO: a facility-scale differentiable virtual accelerator for Fermilab
description: As the design complexity of modern accelerators grows, there is more interest in using advanced simulations that have fast execution time or yield additional insights like gradients. The FAST/IOTA facility has been working on implementing and experimentally validating an end-to-end digital twin that is both fast and gradient-aware, allowing for rapid prototyping of new software and experiments with minimal beam time costs. Our framework integrates physics and ML codes for linac and ring simulation through a set of generic interfaces between surrogate and physics-based sections. To reproduce device inputs and outputs, system state is exposed as a deterministic discrete event simulator. Because Fermilab is undergoing control system transition, both EPICS and ACNET frontends are supported. Recently, we have begun transitioning to a new community lattice standard, PALS, as well as developing standardized infrastructure for data ingest and normalization to prepare for model calibration during FAST proton injector commissioning. We discuss implementation details as well as challenges, and future plans to extend modelling to main complex proton accelerators like PIPII and Booster.

title: From model to application, how to design a user interface

speakers.name: Mihnea Romanovschi
speakers.manual_institution: ISIS Pulsed Neutron and Muon Source, UKRI, STFC

authors.name: Mihnea Romanovschi
authors.manual_institution: ISIS Pulsed Neutron and Muon Source, UKRI, STFC

description: The Controls group at ISIS has been experimenting with Machine Learning (ML) applications to aid in beam optimization, anomaly detection and surrogate modelling. Whilst the development is still ongoing, as we improve our infrastructure and algorithms we have found certain hurdles, especially when it comes to anomaly detection and reporting. This is particularly notable when developing the tools in a form which the end users would find easy to interact with. There is not one generic solution when it comes to designing the user interface for such applications, and different engineers and teams have certain expectations regarding interacting with the system and gaining useful information for maintenance work or urgent operator use. In this talk we will showcase the typical expectations of different types of users. We will show how what features of anomaly detection are desirable and how the user experience needs to adapt for new situations.

title: Latent Laser Pulse Represenations and Surrogate Beam Dynamics for EuXFEL Emittance Optimization
description: Minimizing transverse emittance in the European XFEL photoinjector requires precise control of the longitudinal laser pulse shape, yet exploring the full shaping space through beam dynamics simulations is computationally prohibitive. We address this by learning a low-dimensional, physics-aware latent representation of realistic pulse profiles using a Wasserstein Autoencoder (WAE). The latent space provides a smooth and structured domain for efficient sampling and interpolation of pulse variations.Using this representation, we run large-scale ASTRA simulations of the PITZ injector lattice and map how latent pulse coordinates and machine settings influence projected and slice emittance. We further train a fast surrogate model that predicts beam quality from latent codes and machine parameters, enabling rapid evaluation of candidate configurations and gradient based optimization. Finally, we outline how this latent-space surrogate forms a natural counterpart to ongoing differentiable laser-modeling efforts for EuXFEL, supporting end-to-end optimization of photoinjector emittance.

title: Development of Bayesian optimization system for high intensity heavy ion beams at RIBF

speakers.name: Takahiro Nishi
speakers.manual_institution: RIKEN Nishina Center for Accelerator-Based Science

authors.name: Takahiro Nishi
authors.manual_institution: RIKEN Nishina Center for Accelerator-Based Science

description: Accelerator facilities are controlled by numerous parameters; at the RIKEN RI Beam Factory (RIBF), a complex of cyclotrons and linacs, more than 600 parameters —including environmental factors—affect beam quality. To optimize them more efficiently, we have been developing Bayesian optimization (BO) techniques, focusing on indices suitable for high-intensity beams and methods that maintain operational safety. We established a technique to measure beam transmission and spot size simultaneously using charge-converted particles downstream of the target, and we are also studying line BO with safety functions. We report these developments, focusing on the test results obtained with the heavy ion beam.

title: ML-based Automated Tuning and Operations at SACLA
description: Modern X-ray Free-Electron Lasers (XFELs) are facing a paradigm shift in user requirements, moving from simple intensity maximization to sophisticated control over spatial profiles, spectral characteristics, and temporal stability. To address these complex, high-dimensional optimization challenges, SACLA introduced a Bayesian Optimization (BO) based automated tuning framework in 2020. Unlike manual tuning by experienced operators, our BO system effectively handles the stochastic nature and measurement uncertainties. This framework is now routinely employed in daily operations, enabling consistent delivery of advanced beam properties. This robust ML platform is designed for broad applicability; it has been successfully tested for deployment at other accelerator facilities with minimal modifications. Currently, We are also expanding the scope of this ML ecosystem to include fault prediction, paving the way for fully autonomous facility operation. This presentation will detail the architecture, deployment results at SACLA.

title: A scalable approach to multi-framework differentiable simulations
description: There is a growing interest in differentiable simulations that have fast execution time and yield additional gradient information. Most differentiable codes use a single autodiff or TPSA library and then implement optics and particle tracking logic on top. This approach can be limiting due to performance/compilation/ease of use tradeoffs, especially when wanting to use specific hardware accelerators like TPUs and GPUs. We present a new code for differentiable simulations, JACC (JAX for ACCelerators), that can create an interface for several libraries (Jax, PyTorch, NVIDIA Warp, finite differences) though an intelligent beamline generator. It can also stitch pre-compiled or eager execution kernels. This enables easy debugging, while still enabling performance-critical kernel fusion and distributed execution. An important application is end-to-end differentiable RL, where both Jax and PyTorch codebases are popular. Common Xsuite elements are already implemented, and we provide templates for custom NN/GP models. We present benchmarks including collective effects and apertures on CPU and (multi) GPU runs, concluding with a set of recommended settings.

title: Physics-Informed AI-Based Feedforward Compensation for ID Gap Variations
description: Advanced insertion devices (IDs) introduces strongly nonlinear transverse kicks that depend on gap, phase, and beam position, leading, to orbit distortions that vary with beam trajectory. Conventional lookup table-based feedforward methods assume a fixed orbit and lose accuracy when the beam position changes. In this work, neural network (NN) models are used to predict position-dependent kicks across a wide operating range and are compared with lookup tables. Simulation results show that NN-based methods significantly improve accuracy and robustness, particularly in strongly nonlinear regimes. A physics-informed neural network (PINN) is also explored, but its performance is limited by model tuning in this study. These results highlight the potential of NN-based approaches for improved orbit correction in advanced light sources.

title: Towards reconstruction of full six-dimensional phase spaces for particle accelerators by applying virtual diagnostics to the transverse deflector system
description: Comprehending the six-dimensional phase spaces of particles is one of the ultimate goals for the beam diagnostics in the particle accelerator facilities. As it is, in general, impossible to obtain a phase space of more than two dimensions, it is necessary to estimate the higher dimensional phase space by combining data from several monitors for transverse and longitudinal diagnostics. A generative AI can play a role as virtual diagnostics to reconstruct the full phase spaces of the particles, although it requires a large amount of simulated and measured data in order to train the algorithm. At SACLA, we have been developing the Transverse Deflector System (TDS) for the longitudinal diagnostics of the electron bunch as well as an ecosystem for AIML-friendly simulators of electron beam tracking and XFEL interactions. Training the generative AI using both the simulated and measured data from the TDS will be a method to estimate the six-dimensional phase space of the electrons. In this presentation, we overview the TDS system, a whole method of the virtual diagnostics combined with the TDS, and show a simulation results to reconstruct the six-dimensional phase spaces of the electrons.

presentation_materials.filename: MaLAPA26_Towards reconstruction of full six-dimensional phase spaces for particle accelerators by applying virtual diagnostics to the transverse deflector system.pdf

title: Prospects for the Digital Twins of the Experimental Facilities – an Integration of the Facility Data Management Systems with Intelligent Methods
description: The IRRAD Proton Facility, located on T8 beamline at CERN PS East Hall, is an experimental infrastructure hosting irradiation tests and experiments of various nature. It focuses on the development of equipment and electronics for High-Energy Physics (HEP) experiments. The CERN Highly-Accelerated Mixed Field Facility (CHARM) is located nearby, on the same beamline. It is a facility for testing radiation effects on electronics. Over the years of operation, multiple data-management systems were designed and implemented to operate the facilities and monitor key factors of their performance, leading to numerous improvements. These activities also yielded a significant wealth and variety of data and metadata that were used in successful case studies. We present these existing tools and systems, and the long-term endeavour focusing on the possibility of providing a physics- and AI-based “digital twin” for IRRAD and CHARM. Such a virtual representation of the facility, with continuously updating parameters (based on the constantly incoming streams of data), would be an invaluable system for the prediction and optimisation of small- and medium-sized experiments (SME).

title: Reservoir Computing for Surrogate Models
description: We investigate the application of a reservoir computing architecture to surrogate models which reproduce the response dynamics of the Delivery Ring resonant extraction at Fermilab for the Mu2e experiment. In this architecture, a fixed, randomized recurrent network of nonlinear nodes takes one or more time series inputs. Only the readout is trained; it is a shallow network of dense connections, often only a single layer. We compare performance along several metrics to better-established architectures, as well as training advantages such as fast, stable convergence and data efficiency.

title: Optimization under Uncertainty for Proton Beam Accelerators via Differentiable Robust Model Predictive Control
description: Longitudinal beam profiles are important metrics for monitoring and optimally controlling the proton beam in the Fermilab Recycler Ring (FRR). To generate longitudinal profiles, resistive wall current monitor (RWCM) data are analyzed via tomography, a costly computational transformation. The costly RWCM analysis and systemic uncertainties limit the use of longitudinal data in real-time controls in the FRR. To make real time model predictive control (MPC) possible in the FRR to optimize beam parameters, we first construct a differentiable data-driven model to efficiently map RWCM data to longitudinal distributions. This model employs dimensionality reduction techniques on the longitudinal distributions to make the supervised learning task well-posed. We will demonstrate preliminary results in formulating the problem of controlling beam parameters under uncertainty as a differentiable robust model predictive control problem with end-to-end differentiation through a parameterized optimal control formulation.

title: Conditional modeling and Bayesian Optimization for Improved Injection at the Fermilab Booster
description: The Fermilab accelerator complex delivers high-intensity proton beams to serve the lab’s neutrino, muon, and fixed-target programs. A normal-conducting Linac accelerates H− beam to 400 MeV and injects into the Booster rapid cycling synchrotron via charge exchange, which accelerates protons to 8 GeV. Injection from the Linac into the Booster is a critical area the for high-power performance of the Fermilab proton complex. The Booster is a high-intensity proton ring with extreme space-charge forces which necessitates precise control over the beam losses through the acceleration cycle. The main challenge for the reliability of Booster performance is compensating for drifting conditions in the beam from the Linac, which can drift daily in energy by up to O(1) MeV w.r.t. design. Drifts in Linac orbit and energy must be corrected to match the Booster, while simultaneously accommodating interdependent drifts in transverse and longitudinal beam quality. Operationally, compensation for these changes is addressed by manual tuning of the Linac output energy and/or Booster acceptance, which can be inefficient and time-consuming. This works describes conditional Bayesian Optimization for injection tuning that takes into account the state of Linac beam via information from instrumentation in the injection line (Beam position monitors (BPMs), beam loss monitors (BLMs), wire scanners for transverse profiles (WSs)), as well as RF cavity setting parameters from the Linac.

title: Opportunities of machine learning applications on superconducting linac of European Spallation Source (ESS)
description: The European Spallation Source (ESS) in Lund, Sweden, is a multidisciplinary neutron research facility currently under final stages of construction. It is driven by a superconducting proton linac with multi-megawatt beam power. The full 600 m length of the linac has been successfully commissioned with 870 MeV beam in spring of 2025. Further linac commissioning activities and initial beam delivery on the tungsten target for neutron production are planned throughout 2026. As the facility transitions from the commissioning phase to steady-state user operation, efforts are made towards achieving reliable and stable operation of the accelerator. Among these, is exploration and development of various machine-learning–based tools to improve the machine stability and reduce the beam losses. This work presents the possibilities of applying machine learning techniques in the superconducting linac of ESS, particularly focusing on a pilot project of beam trajectory correction using a fully connected feed-forward neural network. The model is trained on beam position data along the linac simulated for different corrector magnet settings and quadrupole magnet offsets.

title: Current capabilities of FACET-II and future prospects: what would you want out of an AI/ML test facility?
description: The Facility for Advanced Accelerator Experimental Tests II (FACET-II) generates electron beams with the highest peak current in the world. These beams are designed to drive 10 GV/m electric fields in plasmas for wakefield acceleration, study astrophysics in the laboratory and study photon-particle interactions at the Schwinger limit. Recent experiments have demonstrated peak currents in excess of 100 kA. While scientifically interesting, characterizing and controlling these particle beams is highly challenging. Their intensity damages most common diagnostics, and they are sensitive to instabilities and machine drift. This presentation will review the FACET-II facility and its capabilities, and address the associated challenges in beam diagnostics, tuning, and control. It will also provide an overview of the data collection system and ongoing efforts to implement AI/ML techniques to enhance the repeatability and reliability of machine operation at the edge of stability. Community input is sought on how FACET-II and its unique capabilities might be further enhanced and used as a future AI/ML test facility.
